commentary: The liquidity hierarchy clusters into three distinct tiers. Nasdaq 100 and NYSE lead decisively, with ILLIQ costs under 0.0055% per $1M traded and spreads near 0.64–0.75%, anchored by comparable volumes exceeding $230M USD. Euronext, Tokyo Stock Exchange, and Hong Kong Stock Exchange form a solid mid-pack, trading ILLIQ costs of 0.0284–0.0360% per $1M with spreads between 0.61% and 0.79%, though Tokyo's 3.21% turnover ratio substantially exceeds its peers' sub-1% rates, suggesting concentrated activity among a smaller cap base. The laggard tier—Xetra, Korea Exchange, SIX Swiss, and London Stock Exchange—exhibits material deterioration in at least one dimension: Korea Exchange's 1.40% spread and 0.1339% per $1M ILLIQ are particularly acute, while London Stock Exchange posts the highest ILLIQ at 0.0732% per $1M despite a respectable 0.79% spread. SIX Swiss presents an intriguing divergence: its 0.5383% spread ranks tightest globally, yet its minuscule $3.0M comparable volume and 0.1506% turnover ratio signal depth constraints that spreads alone don't capture. This spread-liquidity decoupling warrants caution when executing larger positions in lower-tier venues.
